concerns.text: The plan relies on a 'synthetic dataset' to answer a causal research question ('Which process parameters... influence ductility'). Synthetic data generated with 'realistic ranges and noise' cannot capture the complex, non-linear physical mechanisms or unobserved confounders of real AM processes. A model trained on synthetic data will have zero external validity; it cannot answer the research question regarding real-world materials.

The plan states a dataset size of '≤ 250 records' and a synthetic generation of '~100 records'. For a Linear Mixed-Effects model with 5+ fixed effects and random intercepts across multiple alloy families, this sample size is critically underpowered. With <100 records, the model cannot reliably estimate variance components or detect moderate effect sizes, rendering the statistical inference (p-values, CIs) invalid.

The plan proposes fitting a Linear Mixed-Effects (LME) model and an XGBoost model on the same small dataset. With N < 100, splitting data for XGBoost training/validation/testing (FR-010) will result in test sets too small to yield meaningful performance metrics (R², MAE). The variance of these metrics will be too high to support any conclusion about predictive capability.

The plan includes 'Energy Density' as a predictor alongside its components (Power, Speed, etc.) initially, then drops components if VIF > 5. While VIF handling is correct, the plan does not address the fundamental issue that Energy Density is a deterministic function of the other variables. In a small sample, this mathematical dependency creates a singular design matrix that standard LME solvers may fail to invert, regardless of VIF thresholds, unless the design is explicitly constrained.
